title: Brent crude hits $126 four-year high as Hormuz blockade and Iran war fears intensify
summary: Global oil prices surged to four-year highs with Brent crude hitting $126 per barrel on reports of potential new US military strikes against Iran and the continued closure of the Strait of Hormuz blocking 20% of global oil supply.
contents:
## Oil Prices Spike on Escalation Fears

### Price Action
International benchmark Brent crude surged more than seven percent to $126 per barrel before easing in afternoon trading, marking a four-year high. The spike followed reports that Trump received a briefing on plans for new military strikes against Iran.

### Supply Disruption Scale
The Strait of Hormuz remains closed, choking off approximately 20% of the world's oil and gas supplies. IEA chief Fatih Birol declared: "The world faces the biggest energy crisis in history." The combined effect of Iran's strait closure and the US naval blockade of Iranian ports has created an unprecedented supply shock.

### US Consumer Impact
US gasoline prices now average $4.39 per gallon, the highest since July 2022, with a 33-cent increase in the past week alone. Jet fuel prices nearly doubled in two months, driving up airfares significantly as summer travel season approaches.

### US Blockade Operations
US Central Command has redirected 44 commercial vessels as part of the Iran blockade. The Navy has boarded at least three ships, including a "sanctioned stateless vessel" carrying Iranian oil in the Indian Ocean. The blockade involves 19 ships in the Middle East, including two aircraft carriers.

### Market Outlook
Traders face extreme uncertainty as the ceasefire appears fragile and Iran develops alternative export routes. The disconnect between Iran's defiant rhetoric and economic strain creates volatile market conditions with potential for further upside in energy prices.
